comment_text.snp3.7:2.1: Ke·πáiya features also in the Vinaya, where at this point he offered the Buddha and the Sangha a drink in the evening, reasoning that the Brahmanical sages of the past did likewise (Kd 6:35.1.2). As a result, the Buddha allowed fruit juice and other drinks in the evening.
comment_text.snp3.7:4.8: ‚ÄúManual preparations‚Äù is kƒÅyaveyyƒÅva·π≠ika·πÅ. Ten kinds of service are enumerated in the Jain TattvƒÅrthas≈´tra 9.24.
comment_text.snp3.7:7.7: AnƒÅthapi·πá·∏çika responded in the same way when hearing of the Buddha (Kd 16:4.2.10). Also compare BƒÅvari‚Äôs elation at Snp 5.1:19.1.
comment_text.snp3.7:9.6: DurƒÅsada (‚Äúintimidating‚Äù) is also at AN 4.42:4.1, which shows that the sense is that they are hard to defeat in debate.
comment_text.snp3.7:12.5: This idea is not found elsewhere in the Pali.
comment_text.snp3.7:19.2: While the content of the text is identical with MN 92, the current text has more tags identifying the speakers.
comment_text.snp3.7:19.5: This is of course a reference to the first sermon (SN 56.11).
comment_text.snp3.7:21.1: The title ‚ÄúGeneral of the Dhamma‚Äù belongs to SƒÅriputta (Ud 2.8:16.2, Thag 18.1:33.1).
comment_text.snp3.7:22.5: AnujƒÅta is said at Iti 74:4.1 to be a child who ‚Äútakes after‚Äù the good qualities of their parents.
comment_text.snp3.7:24.4: Abhi·πáhaso means ‚Äúrepeatedly‚Äù. Here the force of the saying is, I think, ‚ÄúIt is hard enough to encounter a Buddha even once, let alone repeatedly.‚Äù
comment_text.snp3.7:28.4: The ‚Äúdark class‚Äù refers to those born in an unfortunate state (AN 6.57:11.1). Sela is saying that the Buddha‚Äôs path is for everyone, not just the fortunate.
comment_text.snp3.7:35.1: Mukha means originally ‚Äúmouth‚Äù, hence ‚Äúopening‚Äù, ‚Äúentrance‚Äù; but also ‚Äúface‚Äù, hence ‚Äúfront‚Äù, ‚Äúforemost‚Äù. The fire is the foremost sacrifice, but fire is also a ‚Äúmouth‚Äù, since ‚Äúhowever much one may put into a fire, it burns it all up all‚Äù (B·πõhadƒÅra·πáyaka Upani·π£ad 5.14.8). This sense of ‚Äúconsume‚Äù or ‚Äúsubsume‚Äù is alive in these verses as well. For example, the SƒÅvitrƒ´ ‚Äúsubsumes‚Äù other hymns, for merely one line is equivalent to all three Vedas (BU 5.14.2). The sense of mukha as ‚Äúentrance, introduction‚Äù also applies, since the fire sacrifice and the SƒÅvitrƒ´ are the things first learned by a new student (≈öatapatha BrƒÅhma·πáa 11.5.4.12). Fire is also the ‚Äúfirst‚Äù creation, and hence the oldest of rituals (≈öB 6.1.1.11).¬Ý | Ya√±√±ƒÅ is a truncated genitive plural (‚Äúof sacrifices‚Äù).
comment_text.snp3.7:35.2: The SƒÅvitrƒ´ is a famous verse recited daily by brahmin priests at dawn: ‚ÄúMight we make our own that desirable effulgence of god Savitar, who will rouse forth our insights (Rig Veda 3.62.10, Jamison/Brereton). It was the ‚Äúhead‚Äù of metres (≈õiro gƒÅyatrya·∏•, ≈öB 8.6.2.3, 8.6.2.6), and was ‚Äúpraised as the foremost of recitations‚Äù (gƒÅyatrƒÅ·πáy uktha mukhanai ≈õa·πÅsanti, Kau·π£ƒ´taki BrƒÅhma·πáa 17.7.1). ‚ÄúPrayers‚Äù, furthermore, are born ‚Äúfrom the mouth‚Äù (mukhato, ≈öB 11.5.4.17). The SƒÅvitrƒ´ is the chief exponent of the gƒÅyatrƒ´ metre, by which it is sometimes known. The three-lined gƒÅyatrƒ´ is the ‚Äúfirst‚Äù of poetic styles, both technically‚Äîas it is the shortest from which others were derived (≈öB 1.3.4.6, 1.6.2.10)‚Äîand historically, as it dominated the Rig Veda but fell out of fashion later. | Chandas can be either ‚Äúmeter‚Äù in general, or a metrical song of praise, especially a Vedic hymn (eg. ≈öB 11.5.7.3). The SƒÅvitrƒ´ is a Vedic text, not a meter, so the latter meaning must apply here. It is a collective singular.
comment_text.snp3.7:40.1: This verse and the next are also at Snp 3.6:49.1.

root_text.snp3.7:38.1: Atha kho ƒÅyasmƒÅ selo sapariso yena bhagavƒÅ tenupasa·πÖkami, upasa·πÖkamitvƒÅ eka·πÅsa·πÅ cƒ´vara·πÅ katvƒÅ yena bhagavƒÅ tena√±jali·πÅ pa·πáƒÅmetvƒÅ bhagavanta·πÅ gƒÅthƒÅya ajjhabhƒÅsi:

translation_text.snp3.7:1.2: At one time the Buddha was wandering together with a large Sa·πÖgha of 1,250 mendicants in the land of the A·πÖguttarƒÅpans when he arrived at a town of theirs named ƒÄpa·πáa.
translation_text.snp3.7:1.4: ‚ÄúIt seems the ascetic Gotama‚Äîa Sakyan, gone forth from a Sakyan family‚Äîhas arrived at ƒÄpa·πáa, together with a large Sa·πÖgha of 1,250 mendicants.
translation_text.snp3.7:1.6: ‚ÄòThat Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.‚Äô
translation_text.snp3.7:1.7: He has realized with his own insight this world‚Äîwith its gods, MƒÅras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans‚Äîand he makes it known to others.
translation_text.snp3.7:1.8: He proclaims a teaching that is good in the beginning, good in the middle, and good in the end, meaningful and well-phrased. And he reveals a spiritual practice that‚Äôs entirely full and pure.
translation_text.snp3.7:1.9: It‚Äôs good to see such perfected ones.‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:2.1: So Ke·πáiya approached the Buddha and exchanged greetings with him.
translation_text.snp3.7:2.2: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side.
translation_text.snp3.7:2.3: The Buddha educated, encouraged, fired up, and inspired him with a Dhamma talk.
translation_text.snp3.7:2.4: Then he said to the Buddha,
translation_text.snp3.7:2.5: ‚ÄúWould Mister Gotama together with the mendicant Sa·πÖgha please accept tomorrow‚Äôs meal from me?‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:2.6: When he said this, the Buddha said to him,
translation_text.snp3.7:2.7: ‚ÄúThe Sa·πÖgha is large, Ke·πáiya; there are 1,250 mendicants. And you are devoted to the brahmins.‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:3.1: For a second time, Ke·πáiya asked the Buddha to accept a meal offering.
translation_text.snp3.7:3.2: ‚ÄúNever mind that the Sa·πÖgha is large, with 1,250 mendicants, and that I am devoted to the brahmins.
translation_text.snp3.7:3.3: Would Mister Gotama together with the mendicant Sa·πÖgha please accept tomorrow‚Äôs meal from me?‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:3.4: And for a second time, the Buddha gave the same reply.
translation_text.snp3.7:4.1: For a third time, Ke·πáiya asked the Buddha to accept a meal offering.
translation_text.snp3.7:4.2: ‚ÄúNever mind that the Sa·πÖgha is large, with 1,250 mendicants, and that I am devoted to the brahmins.
translation_text.snp3.7:4.3: Would Mister Gotama together with the mendicant Sa·πÖgha please accept tomorrow‚Äôs meal from me?‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:4.5: Then, knowing that the Buddha had consented, Ke·πáiya got up from his seat and went to his own hermitage. There he addressed his friends and colleagues, relatives and kin,
translation_text.snp3.7:4.6: ‚ÄúMy friends and colleagues, relatives and kin: please listen!
translation_text.snp3.7:4.7: The ascetic Gotama together with the mendicant Sa·πÖgha has been invited by me for tomorrow‚Äôs meal.
translation_text.snp3.7:4.8: Please help me out with the manual preparations.‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:4.9: ‚ÄúYes, worthy sir,‚Äù they replied. Some dug ovens, some chopped wood, some washed dishes, some set out a water jar, and some spread out seats.
translation_text.snp3.7:4.10: Meanwhile, Ke·πáiya set up the pavilion himself.
translation_text.snp3.7:5.1: Now at that time the brahmin Sela was residing in ƒÄpa·πáa. He had mastered the three Vedas, together with their vocabularies and ritual performance, their phonology and word classification, and the testaments as fifth. He knew them word-by-word, and their grammar. He was well versed in cosmology and the marks of a great man. And he was teaching three hundred young students to recite the hymns.
translation_text.snp3.7:6.1: And at that time Ke·πáiya was devoted to Sela.
translation_text.snp3.7:6.2: Then Sela, while going for a walk escorted by the three hundred young students, approached Ke·πáiya‚Äôs hermitage.
translation_text.snp3.7:6.3: He saw the preparations going on,
translation_text.snp3.7:6.5: ‚ÄúKe·πáiya, is your son or daughter being married? Or are you setting up a big sacrifice? Or has King Seniya BimbisƒÅra of Magadha been invited for tomorrow‚Äôs meal?‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:7.1: ‚ÄúThere is no marriage, Sela, and the king is not coming.
translation_text.snp3.7:7.2: Rather, I am setting up a big sacrifice.
translation_text.snp3.7:7.3: The ascetic Gotama has arrived at ƒÄpa·πáa, together with a large Sa·πÖgha of 1,250 mendicants.
translation_text.snp3.7:7.5: ‚ÄòThat Blessed One is perfected, a fully awakened Buddha, accomplished in knowledge and conduct, holy, knower of the world, supreme guide for those fit for training, teacher of gods and humans, awakened, blessed.‚Äô
translation_text.snp3.7:7.6: He has been invited by me for tomorrow‚Äôs meal together with the mendicant Sa·πÖgha.‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:7.7: ‚ÄúWorthy Ke·πáiya, did you say ‚Äòthe awakened one‚Äô?‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:7.9: ‚ÄúWorthy Ke·πáiya, did you say ‚Äòthe awakened one‚Äô?‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:8.2: ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs hard to even find the word ‚Äòawakened one‚Äô in the world.
translation_text.snp3.7:8.3: The thirty-two marks of a great man have been handed down in our hymns. A great man who possesses these has only two possible destinies, no other.
translation_text.snp3.7:8.4: If he stays at home he becomes a king, a wheel-turning monarch, a just and principled king. His dominion extends to all four sides, he achieves stability in the country, and he possesses the seven treasures.
translation_text.snp3.7:8.5: He has the following seven treasures:
translation_text.snp3.7:8.6: the wheel, the elephant, the horse, the jewel, the woman, the householder, and the commander as the seventh treasure.
translation_text.snp3.7:8.7: He has over a thousand sons who are valiant and heroic, crushing the armies of his enemies.
translation_text.snp3.7:8.8: After conquering this land girt by sea, he reigns by principle, without rod or sword.
translation_text.snp3.7:8.9: But if he goes forth from the lay life to homelessness, he becomes a perfected one, a fully awakened Buddha, who has cast off the world‚Äôs veil.
translation_text.snp3.7:8.10: ‚ÄúBut Ke·πáiya, where is the Blessed One at present, the perfected one, the fully awakened Buddha?‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:9.1: When he said this, Ke·πáiya pointed with his right arm and said,
translation_text.snp3.7:9.2: ‚ÄúThere, worthy Sela, at that line of blue forest.‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:9.3: Then Sela, together with his young students, approached the Buddha.
translation_text.snp3.7:9.4: He said to his young students,
translation_text.snp3.7:9.6: For the Buddhas are intimidating, like a lion living alone.
translation_text.snp3.7:9.7: When I‚Äôm consulting with the ascetic Gotama, don‚Äôt interrupt.
translation_text.snp3.7:10.1: Then Sela went up to the Buddha, and exchanged greetings with him.
translation_text.snp3.7:10.2: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side.
translation_text.snp3.7:10.3: and scrutinized the Buddha‚Äôs body for the thirty-two marks of a great man.
translation_text.snp3.7:10.4: He saw all of them except for two,
translation_text.snp3.7:10.6: whether the private parts were covered in a foreskin, and the largeness of the tongue.
translation_text.snp3.7:11.1: Then it occurred to the Buddha,
translation_text.snp3.7:11.2: ‚ÄúSela sees all the marks except for two,
translation_text.snp3.7:11.4: whether the private parts are covered in a foreskin, and the largeness of the tongue.‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:11.5: The Buddha used his psychic power to will that Sela would see his private parts covered in a foreskin.
translation_text.snp3.7:11.6: And he stuck out his tongue and stroked back and forth on his ear holes and nostrils, and covered his entire forehead with his tongue.
translation_text.snp3.7:12.2: ‚ÄúThe ascetic Gotama possesses the thirty-two marks completely, lacking none.
translation_text.snp3.7:12.3: But I don‚Äôt know whether or not he is an awakened one.
translation_text.snp3.7:12.4: I have heard that brahmins of the past who were elderly and senior, the tutors of tutors, said,
translation_text.snp3.7:12.5: ‚ÄòThose who are perfected ones, fully awakened Buddhas reveal themselves when praised.‚Äô
translation_text.snp3.7:12.6: Why don‚Äôt I extoll him in his presence with fitting verses?‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:12.7: Then Sela extolled the Buddha in his presence with fitting verses:
translation_text.snp3.7:13.2: you‚Äôre radiant, handsome, lovely to behold;
translation_text.snp3.7:13.4: with teeth so white; you‚Äôre strong.
translation_text.snp3.7:14.3: the marks of a great man,
translation_text.snp3.7:14.4: are all found on your body.
translation_text.snp3.7:15.1: Your eyes are clear, your face is fair,
translation_text.snp3.7:15.3: In the midst of the Sa·πÖgha of ascetics,
translation_text.snp3.7:16.1: You‚Äôre a mendicant fine to see,
translation_text.snp3.7:16.2: with skin that shines like lustrous gold.
translation_text.snp3.7:16.4: what do you want with the ascetic life?
translation_text.snp3.7:17.1: You‚Äôre fit to be a king,
translation_text.snp3.7:17.2: a wheel-turning monarch, chief of charioteers,
translation_text.snp3.7:17.4: lord of the Black Plum Tree Land.
translation_text.snp3.7:18.4: as king of kings, lord of mankind!‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:19.4: By the teaching I roll forth the wheel
translation_text.snp3.7:21.2: the disciple who follows the Teacher‚Äôs way?
translation_text.snp3.7:21.4: of the teaching you rolled forth?‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:22.1: ‚ÄúBy me the wheel was rolled forth,‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:22.4: SƒÅriputta, taking after the Realized One,
translation_text.snp3.7:23.1: I have known what should be known,
translation_text.snp3.7:23.2: and developed what should be developed,
translation_text.snp3.7:23.3: and given up what should be given up:
translation_text.snp3.7:23.4: and so, brahmin, I am a Buddha.
translation_text.snp3.7:25.3: one of those whose appearance in the world
translation_text.snp3.7:26.4: I rejoice, fearing nothing from any quarter.‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:27.2: is spoken by the Clear-eyed One.
translation_text.snp3.7:27.4: roars like a lion in the jungle.
translation_text.snp3.7:28.3: who would not be inspired by him,
translation_text.snp3.7:28.4: even one born in a dark class?
translation_text.snp3.7:29.1: Those who wish may follow me;
translation_text.snp3.7:29.3: Right here, I‚Äôll go forth in his presence,
translation_text.snp3.7:29.4: the one of such outstanding wisdom.‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:30.3: we‚Äôll also go forth in his presence,
translation_text.snp3.7:30.4: the one of such outstanding wisdom.‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:31.2: with joined palms held up, ask:
translation_text.snp3.7:32.3: ‚Äúapparent in the present life, immediately effective.
translation_text.snp3.7:32.4: Here the going forth isn‚Äôt in vain
translation_text.snp3.7:32.5: for one who trains with diligence.‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:33.1: And the brahmin Sela together with his assembly received the going forth, the ordination in the Buddha‚Äôs presence.
translation_text.snp3.7:33.2: And when the night had passed Ke·πáiya had delicious fresh and cooked foods prepared in his own hermitage. Then he had the Buddha informed of the time, saying,
translation_text.snp3.7:33.3: ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs time, worthy Gotama, the meal is ready.‚Äù
translation_text.snp3.7:33.4: Then the Buddha robed up in the morning and, taking his bowl and robe, went to Ke·πáiya‚Äôs hermitage, where he sat on the seat spread out, together with the Sa·πÖgha of mendicants.
translation_text.snp3.7:34.1: Then Ke·πáiya served and satisfied the mendicant Sa·πÖgha headed by the Buddha with his own hands with delicious fresh and cooked foods.
translation_text.snp3.7:34.2: When the Buddha had eaten and washed his hand and bowl, Ke·πáiya took a low seat and sat to one side.
translation_text.snp3.7:34.3: The Buddha expressed his appreciation with these verses:
translation_text.snp3.7:35.1: ‚ÄúThe foremost of sacrifices <j>is the offering to the sacred flame;
translation_text.snp3.7:35.2: the SƒÅvitrƒ´ is the foremost of prayer;
translation_text.snp3.7:35.3: of humans, the king is the foremost;
translation_text.snp3.7:35.4: the ocean‚Äôs the foremost of rivers;
translation_text.snp3.7:36.1: the foremost of stars is the moon;
translation_text.snp3.7:36.2: the sun is the foremost of blazes;
translation_text.snp3.7:36.3: for those who sacrifice seeking merit,
translation_text.snp3.7:37.1: When the Buddha had expressed his appreciation to Ke·πáiya the matted-hair ascetic with these verses, he got up from his seat and left.
translation_text.snp3.7:37.2: Then Venerable Sela and his assembly, living alone, withdrawn, diligent, keen, and resolute, soon realized the supreme end of the spiritual path in this very life. They lived having achieved with their own insight the goal for which gentlemen rightly go forth from the lay life to homelessness.
translation_text.snp3.7:37.3: And Venerable Sela together with his assembly became perfected.
translation_text.snp3.7:38.1: Then Sela with his assembly went to see the Buddha. He arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his joined palms toward the Buddha, and said:
translation_text.snp3.7:39.2: we went for refuge, O Clear-eyed One.
translation_text.snp3.7:39.3: In these seven days, Blessed One,
translation_text.snp3.7:39.4: we‚Äôve become tamed in your teaching.
translation_text.snp3.7:40.1: You are the Buddha, you are the Teacher,
translation_text.snp3.7:40.2: you are the sage who has overcome MƒÅra;
translation_text.snp3.7:40.3: you have cut off the underlying tendencies,
translation_text.snp3.7:40.4: you‚Äôve crossed over, and you bring humanity across.
translation_text.snp3.7:41.3: you are a lion, free of grasping,
translation_text.snp3.7:41.4: with fear and dread given up.
translation_text.snp3.7:42.3: Stretch out your feet, great hero:
translation_text.snp3.7:42.4: let these giants bow to the Teacher.‚Äù
